findings.description: Top letterhead layout is simplified and reflowed in HTML.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the state seal/logo appears at upper left with agency title beside it and officials listed in a right-side block separated by a vertical rule. In the HTML, the header content is stacked/reflowed into a simpler single-column arrangement with much more whitespace and without the same side-by-side structure.

findings.description: Agency seal/logo image is missing from the HTML conversion.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows the NC Department of Health and Human Services circular seal/logo in the upper-left header. The HTML header contains only text and no corresponding image.

findings.description: Header typography and alignment are not fully preserved.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses a more formal letterhead treatment with distinct bolded names aligned in a right-hand block and agency branding beside the logo; the HTML renders these elements with simpler text styling and different alignment, reducing visual hierarchy though content remains readable.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is not faithful to the document structure. The HTML uses only h3 elements for table section titles and has no single document title heading; it also nests multiple <main> elements, which weakens structural fidelity.
findings.evidence:
HTML uses headings such as "

Claims Accuracy and Timeliness Review: Summary Findings

", "

Solvency Review: Current Ratio Summary Findings

", "

Solvency Review: Total Expenses to Total Medicaid Revenue Summary Findings

", and "

Solvency Review: Defensive Interval Summary Findings

" with no h1/h2 title structure. In the PDF, these appear as major section/table titles across pages rather than subordinate third-level headings.
